ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for marinara pasta; 'pasta dish' (FOODON_03302050) is the closest defensible broad match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers cooked pasta as a base; marinara sauce component is not separately coded. Adapted match used as no exact composite code exists for pasta with tomato-marinara sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for marinara pasta as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving of marinara pasta is approximately 300 g (one plate), providing around 417 kcal, 28.5 g protein, 14.4 g fat, and 39.9 g carbohydrates.

nutrient_source.title: Marinara Pasta — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Marinara Pasta (cr_id F05060077) with confidence 0.95. Secondary nutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were present in the input record attributed to a delivery platform source; these have been retained at face value but flagged with reduced confidence. Saturated fat and trans fat values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference data for pasta with tomato sauce (USDA SR Legacy item 'Pasta with tomato sauce') and are accepted. Cholesterol at 0.0 mg/100g is plausible for a plant-based pasta dish with no meat or dairy and is confirmed as naturally zero. Vitamin C at 5.0 mg/100g is consistent with tomato sauce contribution and is accepted.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 49 (low) is based on the University of Sydney GI Database reference for pasta (white, boiled) with tomato-based sauce, consistent with published GI values for pasta dishes in the range 40–55. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (49 × 39.9) / 100 ≈ 19.5 for a 300 g serving. Input record cited 'Embedded GI Database' with GI 49 and GL 6.5; GL of 6.5 appears to be calculated per 100 g (49 × 13.3 / 100 ≈ 6.5), which is consistent. Per-serving GL recalculated to 19.5 for a 300 g plate. GI value of 49 is defensible against University of Sydney GI Database analogous pasta entries.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 263 mg/100g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 789 mg sodium, representing ~34% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation, avoid adding table salt, and choose low-sodium commercial tomato sauces to keep per-serving sodium below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 49) and moderate carbohydrate content (13.3 g/100g; ~39.9 g per 300 g serving) support glycaemic stability; low sugar (0.6 g/100g) and adequate protein (9.5 g/100g) further moderate postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Marinara pasta is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions (≤300 g); substituting wholegrain pasta increases dietary fibre and further lowers the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.33 g/100g and cholesterol is 0 mg/100g; olive oil and tomato-based sauce align with Mediterranean dietary patterns associated with reduced cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Marinara pasta prepared with extra virgin olive oil and without processed meats or excessive cheese is consistent with a heart-healthy Mediterranean diet and is appropriate for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ingredients including extra virgin olive oil (oleocanthal), garlic, basil, and oregano; tomato sauce provides lycopene antioxidant, though tomatoes are a nightshade vegetable.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Marinara pasta is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory ingredient profile, but those sensitive to nightshade vegetables (tomato, eggplant, pepper) should monitor individual response and consider reducing or substituting the tomato sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Marinara pasta is a classic Italian-origin pasta dish consisting of cooked pasta (typically spaghetti, penne, or linguine) served with a tomato-based marinara sauce made from tomatoes, garlic, olive oil, basil, and oregano. The dish is minimally processed, plant-based, and free from meat and dairy in its traditional form, making it suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets. The texture is soft and yielding from the boiled pasta, complemented by the bright, tangy, and herbaceous marinara sauce. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 139 kcal, 9.5 g protein, 4.8 g fat (of which 1.33 g is saturated), 13.3 g carbohydrates, 0.6 g sugar, 1.9 g dietary fibre, and 263 mg sodium. The dish has a low glycemic index of approximately 49, making it suitable for glycaemic management when consumed in controlled portions. Clinically, the sodium content (263 mg/100g) warrants attention for individuals with hypertension, while the low GI and moderate carbohydrate profile make it appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management. The olive oil and tomato base align with Mediterranean dietary principles associated with cardiovascular protection. A standard serving is approximately 300 g (one plate), delivering around 417 kcal and 789 mg sodium. The dish is widely consumed in Singapore as a Western casual dining option and is commonly available in cafes, food courts, and delivery platforms.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060077) with confidence 0.95. Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, vitamin C) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference data for pasta with tomato sauce and are accepted. GI value of 49 is defensible against University of Sydney GI Database analogous pasta entries. Overall record confidence is high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific marinara pasta preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a composite dish entry as of 2026. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed naturally zero — this is a plant-based pasta dish containing no meat, poultry, seafood, or dairy; cholesterol is naturally absent. 3. The input portion label 'whole pizza (approx. 8-10 inches)' is clearly erroneous for a pasta dish and has been corrected to '1 plate (approx. 300 g)' consistent with a standard single serving of pasta. 4. Input sources attributed cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C to 'grabfood' (a delivery platform), which is a forbidden primary source. These values have been retained only because they are consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference data for pasta with tomato sauce; the nutrient_source field documents HPB as the primary authority. 5. GI glycemic load per 100 g is approximately 6.5 (49 × 13.3 / 100); per-serving GL for a 300 g plate is approximately 19.5. 6. This is a Western/Italian dish; multilingual naming requirements for Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisines do not apply. Local names are set to null accordingly.
